question: What is the fail-stop assumption in the context of database system crashes?
mock_answer: The fail-stop assumption is the belief that when hardware errors or software bugs occur, they will cause the system to halt completely but will not corrupt the contents of non-volatile storage.
rubric: Must state that the assumption applies to hardware errors or software bugs.
Must state that these errors cause the system to halt.
Must state that the contents of non-volatile storage are assumed to remain uncorrupted.
gold_chunks: System crash . There is a hardware malfunction, or a bug in the database software or the operating system, that causes the loss of the content of volatile storage and brings transaction processing to a halt.
The content of non-volatile storage remains intact and is not corrupted.
The assumption that hardware errors and bugs in the software bring the system to a halt, but do not corrupt the non-volatile storage contents, is known as the failstop assumption .

question: What are the four fields contained in an update log record?
mock_answer: An update log record contains four fields: a transaction identifier, a data-item identifier, the old value of the data item before the write, and the new value of the data item after the write.
rubric: Must list all four fields: transaction identifier, data-item identifier, old value, and new value.
gold_chunks: An update log record describes a single database write.
It has these fields:
Transaction identifier , which is the unique identifier of the transaction that performed the write operation.
Data-item identifier , which is the unique identifier of the data item written.
Old value , which is the value of the data item prior to the write.
New value , which is the value that the data item will have after the write.

question: What are the `redo` and `undo` operations in a log-based recovery system?
mock_answer: In log-based recovery, the `redo` operation uses a log record to set a data item to its new value, while the `undo` operation uses a log record to set a data item back to its old value.
rubric: Must define the `redo` operation as setting a data item to its new value from the log record.
Must define the `undo` operation as setting a data item to its old value from the log record.
gold_chunks: The undo operation using a log record sets the data item specified in the log record to the old value contained in the log record.
The redo operation using a log record sets the data item specified in the log record to the new value contained in the log record.

question: After a system crash, how does a recovery system use the log to determine which transactions to `undo` and which to `redo`?
mock_answer: After a system crash, the recovery system consults the log to ensure atomicity. A transaction needs to be undone if the log contains its `<Ti start>` record but lacks either a `<Ti commit>` or `<Ti abort>` record. Conversely, a transaction needs to be redone if the log contains its `<Ti start>` record and also contains either a `<Ti commit>` or `<Ti abort>` record.
rubric: Must state that the system consults the log after a crash.
Must correctly state the condition for undoing a transaction (start record exists, but no commit or abort record).
Must correctly state the condition for redoing a transaction (start record exists, along with either a commit or abort record).
gold_chunks: After a system crash has occurred, the system consults the log to determine which transactions need to be redone and which need to be undone so as to ensure atomicity.
Transaction T i needs to be undone if the log contains the record < T i start > but does not contain either the record < T i commit > or the record < T i abort > .
Transaction T i needs to be redone if the log contains the record < T i start > and either the record < T i commit > or the record < T i abort > .

composites: Transaction T i needs to be undone if the log contains the record < T i start > but does not contain either the record < T i commit > or the record < T i abort > .
Transaction T i needs to be redone if the log contains the record < T i start > and either the record < T i commit > or the record < T i abort > .

question: Explain the three steps of a simple checkpoint procedure and describe how using checkpoints streamlines the recovery process.
mock_answer: A simple checkpoint procedure involves three steps: 1) writing all log records from main memory to stable storage, 2) writing all modified buffer blocks to disk, and 3) writing a `<checkpoint L>` log record to stable storage, where L is the list of active transactions. Checkpoints streamline recovery because any transaction that completed before the checkpoint is guaranteed to have its modifications written to the database. Therefore, during recovery, the system only needs to find the last checkpoint record and apply redo or undo operations to the transactions that were active at the time of the checkpoint (in list L) and any transactions that started after it. This avoids a full scan of the log and prevents the redoing of already-persisted updates.
rubric: Must list the three steps of the checkpoint procedure: outputting log records, outputting modified buffer blocks, and outputting the checkpoint record.
Must explain that checkpoints allow the recovery system to avoid redoing transactions that committed before the checkpoint.
Must state that recovery only needs to consider transactions from the last checkpoint forward, reducing the amount of log that needs to be processed.
gold_chunks: A checkpoint is performed as follows:
1. Output onto stable storage all log records currently residing in main memory.
2. Output to the disk all modified buffer blocks.
3. Output onto stable storage a log record of the form < checkpoint L>, where L is a list of transactions active at the time of the checkpoint.
The presence of a < checkpoint L> record in the log allows the system to stream-line its recovery procedure.
Consider a transaction T i that completed prior to the checkpoint.
Any database modifications made by T i must have been written to the database either prior to the checkpoint or as part of the checkpoint itself.
Thus, at recovery time, there is no need to perform a redo operation on T i .

After a system crash has occurred, the system examines the log to find the last < checkpoint L> record (this can be done by searching the log backward, from the end of the log, until the first < checkpoint L> record is found).
The redo or undo operations need to be applied only to transactions in L , and to all transactions that started execution after the < checkpoint L> record was written to the log.

question: Why must an `undo` operation generate its own special log records, called compensation log records, and how does this design ensure correct recovery for an aborted transaction if a system crash occurs?
mock_answer: An `undo` operation, which restores data items to their old values, is itself a series of database modifications. To ensure these modifications are durable in case of a system crash during the rollback, they must be logged. The `undo` process writes special redo-only log records, called compensation log records, for each value it restores. Once the `undo` is complete, it writes an `<Ti abort>` log record. The recovery algorithm specifies that any transaction with an `<Ti abort>` record in the log must be redone. If a crash occurs, redoing the aborted transaction involves replaying its log records, which now include the compensation log records. This ensures that the `undo` operation is fully completed, effectively restoring the database to its state before the aborted transaction's modifications, even if the rollback process itself was interrupted by the crash.
rubric: Must identify that an `undo` operation performs database modifications by restoring old values.
Must state that these modifications are logged via compensation log records to ensure they can be recovered after a crash.
Must explain that an `<Ti abort>` record is written upon completion of the `undo`.
Must connect this to the recovery rule that transactions with an `<Ti abort>` record are redone.
Must conclude that redoing the aborted transaction means replaying the compensation log records, thus ensuring the `undo` operation completes.
gold_chunks: The undo operation not only restores the data items to their old value, but also writes log records to record the updates performed as part of the undo process.

These log records are special redo-only log records, since they do not need to contain the old value of the updated data item; note that when such log records are used during undo, the 'old value' is actually the value written by the transaction that is being rolled back, and the 'new value' is the original value that is being restored by the undo operation.
When the undo operation for transaction T i completes, it writes a < T i abort > log record, indicating that the undo has completed.
Transaction T i needs to be redone if the log contains the record < T i start > and either the record < T i commit > or the record < T i abort > .
It may seem strange to redo T i if the record < T i abort > is in the log.
To see why this works, note that if <T i abort > is in the log, so are the redo-only records written by the undo operation.
Thus, the end result will be to undo T i 's modifications in this case.
A special redo-only log record < T i , X j , V 1 > is written to the log, where V 1 is the value being restored to data item Xj during the rollback.
These log records are sometimes called compensation log records .

composites: The undo operation not only restores the data items to their old value, but also writes log records to record the updates performed as part of the undo process.
When the undo operation for transaction T i completes, it writes a < T i abort > log record, indicating that the undo has completed.
Transaction T i needs to be redone if the log contains the record < T i start > and either the record < T i commit > or the record < T i abort > .
To see why this works, note that if <T i abort > is in the log, so are the redo-only records written by the undo operation.
